title: The Effects of Real-Time Feedback on Effort and Performance: Evidence from a Natural Quasi-Experiment
abstract: New digital technologies allow companies to provide employees with performance feedback in real time. We investigate the causal effects of such real-time feedback on individual effort and the quality of initial (prefeedback) and final (postfeedback) decisions by exploiting a natural quasi-experiment in the setting of professional soccer refereeing. Our results suggest that real-time feedback has differential effects for experienced compared with inexperienced agents. Specifically, we find some evidence that experienced agents decrease their effort under real-time feedback without harming the quality of their initial decisions. This results in better final decisions because real-time feedback allows agents to correct wrong initial decisions upon feedback. In contrast, inexperienced agents increase their effort, but make worse initial decisions. Upon feedback, they manage to compensate for their increased number of wrong initial decisions, but see no improvement in the quality of final decisions.

author.Maximilian Margolin: Rotterdam School of Management, Erasmus University Rotterdam, 3000 DR Rotterdam, Netherlands
author.Marko Reimer: Institute of Management Accounting and Control, WHU – Otto Beisheim School of Management, 56179 Vallendar, Germany
author.Daniel Schaupp: Institute of Management Accounting and Control, WHU – Otto Beisheim School of Management, 56179 Vallendar, Germany

article.1. Introduction: In order to facilitate decision support and control, companies generally provide employees with regular performance feedback (Luckett and Eggleton 1991,Lourenço 2015,Casas-Arce et al. 2017a,Merchant and Van der Stede 2017). With the major rise in the adoption and use of digital technologies in recent years, practice has seen a shift away fromtraditionalannual feedback cycles and toward the introduction of systems of continuous, real-time feedback (Baldassarre and Finken 2015,Cappelli and Tavis 2016). Such systems make use of automated data collection and analysis to provide employees with comprehensive feedback on their decisions and performance (nearly) in real time.1Notably, such real-time feedback systems are not limited to blue-collar work where tasks are relatively easy to analyze. Instead, a growing number of companies such as IBM, GE, and Microsoft have reportedly started introducing real-time feedback systems that combine objective performance data with subjective feedback collected from coworkers through, for example, smartphone applications, to support their white-collar workforce in everyday decision making (Baldassarre and Finken 2015,Cappelli and Tavis 2016,Ewenstein et al. 2016). A key benefit of real-time feedback is that employees gain the possibility to promptly change an initial course of action upon feedback (Lurie and Swaminathan 2009,Segupta 2012,Baldassarre and Finken 2015). Traditionally, performance feedback is provided with the aim of improving performance in future decisions. However, in any context where an initial decision can be changed before its consequences fully manifest, real-time feedback creates a second decision moment after the initial decision and the feedback. Thereby, real-time feedback not only serves the purpose oftraditionalfeedback of motivating individuals and improving future decisions, but also allows them to improve the very decision the feedback refers to. Although the creation of a second decision moment is an intriguing new possibility in itself, it raises an important question, namely whether (and if so, how) the prospect of receiving feedback after every decision affects how individuals make the initial decision. In this study, we examine the effects of real-time feedback on agents’ effort and decision quality. Because real-time feedback creates the possibility to correct an initial suboptimal decision upon feedback, it can be expected to result in better final (i.e., postfeedback) decisions. However, the expected effects of real-time feedback on individuals’ effort and quality of initial (i.e., prefeedback) decisions are less clear. We consider two perspectives on real-time feedback which would lead to different predictions regarding its effect on effort and the quality of initial decisions. On the one hand, agents could view real-time feedback as aspotlighton their decision making. Because it provides feedback on every decision, real-time feedback can be perceived as intense scrutiny of the agent’s decision making and thereby trigger feelings of anxiety and worry (Leitenberg 1990,Zeidner and Matthews 2005). Furthermore, real-time feedback can increase the costs associated with making wrong initial decisions because wrong decisions would trigger negative feedback that can harm agents’ self-confidence and reputation (Ashford and Cummings 1983,Northcraft and Ashford 1990,VandeWalle et al. 2000). As a result, we would expect that under real-time feedback agents will exert higher effort in an attempt to avoid the costs associated with wrong initial decisions. At the same time, agents will experience feelings of anxiety and worry due to the perceived intense scrutiny, which can shift attention from on-task activities to threat-related stimuli and thus interfere with the agents’ cognitive processing, eventually resulting in worse initial decisions (Eysenck and Calvo 1992,Eysenck et al. 2007). On the other hand, agents could view real-time feedback as asafety netagainst wrong initial decisions. Because real-time feedback offers the possibility to promptly adjust an initial decision before its consequences (fully) manifest, it allows agents to mitigate (at least some of) the costs associated with making a wrong initial decision. From the perspective of asafety net, real-time feedback would thereby not only reduce the costs of a wrong initial decision but, as a result, also alleviate agents’ anxiety and worry caused by the pressure to make a correct initial decision. Consequently, we would expect that under real-time feedback agents will exert lower effort because their existing incentives to make correct initial decisions are muted. At the same time, agents’ feelings of anxiety and worry associated with making the decision will be reduced, which will benefit their cognitive processing and result in better initial decisions. Following the two perspectives, the effect of real-time feedback on effort and the quality of initial decisions can be positive or negative depending on how an agent perceives it. This notion is consistent with prior research that highlights the importance of contextual factors for how agents will perceive and use feedback (Andiola 2014,Anseel et al. 2015,Ashford et al. 2016). We posit that an agent’s task experience, that is, experience with the decision-making task at hand, will drive their view of real-time feedback as aspotlightorsafety net. Individuals with little task experience are comparably more likely to suffer from anxiety and pressure when being evaluated or expecting negative feedback because they typically lack an established reputation and face greater concerns about others’ impressions of their expertise (Leary and Kowalski 1990,Levy et al. 1995,Canal-Bruland et al. 2010,Anseel et al. 2015,Andiola et al. 2019). As a result, inexperienced agents will perceive a strong pressure to avoid negative feedback, and the view of real-time feedback as aspotlightwill be more salient to them. In contrast, experienced agents with an established reputation will be comparably less likely to suffer from anxiety and pressure to avoid potentially negative feedback, making the view of real-time feedback as asafety netmore salient to them. Consequently, we predict that real-time feedback will have a positive (negative) effect on effort and a negative (positive) effect on the quality of initial decisions for inexperienced (experienced) agents. To empirically examine our predictions, we study the introduction of the Video Assistant Referee (VAR) system in German professional soccer.2The VAR introduction changed the performance feedback referees receive from periodically (typically after every match) to instantly after every important decision, allowing for initially wrong calls to be reversed. This setting provides a unique opportunity to study the effects of real-time feedback for at least two reasons. First, unlike traditional corporate settings, it allows us to observe the complete chain of individuals’ effort, their initial decisions (before feedback), and their final decisions (after feedback). We capture effort by measuring referees’ physical effort during games, as research in sports suggests that physical effort is central to referees’ decision-making quality because it helps them keep up with the gameplay as well as position themselves with a favorable view (Oudejans et al. 2005,Mallo et al. 2012,Gomez-Carmona and Pino-Ortega 2016). Furthermore, because we can observe the calls referees make throughout a game, this allows us to observe the quality of the referees’ initial decisions before feedback, and the quality of their final decisions after feedback. Second, this setting allows us to identify causal effects by exploiting a quasi-experimental research design. The VAR technology was initially introduced only in the 1stGerman Soccer Division, whereas referees from the same pool are assigned to games in the 1stand 2ndDivisions. This allows us to treat games in the 1stDivision as a quasi-experimental treatment group and games in the 2ndDivision as a control group. Our results show support for differential effects of real-time feedback for agents with different levels of task experience. For referees with more experience officiating games, we find some evidence of lower effort in VAR games although the quality of their initial game calls does not seem to deteriorate. Overall, they make better final calls because the VAR enables them to reverse wrong initial calls. In contrast, we find that less experienced referees increase their effort but make worse initial calls in VAR games. Because the VAR does not perfectly correct all referee mistakes, it only compensates for this increased number of wrong initial calls. It does not, however, result in a significant improvement of the quality of final calls made by less experienced referees relative to no-VAR games. In additional analyses, we show that the worse initial calls by less experienced referees are predominantly errors by commission, that is, wrong proactive interruptions of the game, as well as wrong calls in favor of away teams. Both findings are consistent with the notion that less experienced referees view the real-time feedback system as aspotlighton their decision making, leading to pressure on agents in VAR games to engage in impression management strategies such as choosing action rather than inaction to signal determination and decisiveness (Zeelenberg et al. 2002) and making calls distinctly against the common stereotype of a home team bias in referee decision making. Furthermore, we find in additional analyses that the effect of the VAR on referees’ effort and decision quality varies over time. Specifically, we find that less experienced referees’ reactions of increased effort and worse initial decisions are concentrated in games that take place in the first half of the VAR season, which is consistent with the notion that less experienced referees initially react strongly to the perceived scrutiny by the real-time feedback system but eventually learn to work under the system over time. For more experienced referees, we find that their reactions of decreased effort and improved final decisions are concentrated in games that take place in the second half of the VAR season, consistent with the idea that more experienced referees gradually adopt the view of the VAR as asafety netas they gain experience working under the system. Our study contributes to the literature in several ways. First, we contribute to the literature on the performance effects of feedback by documenting the effects of a novel type of feedback, that is, real-time feedback, on individuals’ effort, as well as the quality of their initial (prefeedback) and final (postfeedback) decisions. Our results suggest that the expectation of receiving immediate feedback, which allows agents to correct a wrong initial decision, affects how they make the initial decision. Our results also highlight the importance of differentiating between decision quality, that is, the quality of an individual’s initial decisions before feedback, and overall performance, that is, the quality of an individual’s decisions after feedback and the possibility of changing an initial decision, in a context where a suboptimal initial decision can be corrected before negative consequences manifest (Thornock 2016). Second, we contribute to the discussion of contextual factors of the role of feedback in decision making (Campbell et al. 2011,Casas-Arce et al. 2017b) by highlighting the importance of task experience for the performance effect of real-time feedback. Our results suggest that real-time feedback can have differential effects for agents with different levels of task experience, and specifically that the negative effects of real-time feedback on decision quality can be mitigated by task experience. Notably, in our setting, we find no positive performance effect of real-time feedback with respect to the quality of initial decisions, even for individuals with high task experience. Third, we contribute to research that focuses on technology-based facilitation and control of decision making. Prior research has demonstrated the usefulness of technology-based ex ante decision aids when combined with the possibility of applying discretionary changes for decision making (Dietvorst et al. 2018,Costello et al. 2020). We extend this discussion on the usefulness of technology-based decision aids by examining a real-time feedback system as an example of an ex post decision aid that provides information immediately after every important decision. Our results suggest that such ex post decision aids can be beneficial if they allow for the correction of wrong decisions before their potentially negative consequences (fully) manifest. However, our results caution that the expectation to receive decision-supporting information after every decision can have both beneficial and detrimental effects on initial decision making and behavior.
article.2. Conceptual Background and Hypotheses: Akin totraditionalfeedback, a real-time feedback system provides individuals with information on the outcomes of their actions or decisions. However, a real-time feedback system is different in that it generates and provides this information with little to no time delay after the respective action or decision. As a result, real-time feedback differs fromtraditionalperiodical feedback through (1) a generally higher frequency and timeliness of the feedback the individual receives and, as a result, (2) the possibility to react to feedback more promptly. In any context where a decision can be subsequently changed before its consequences fully manifest, real-time feedback thus creates a second decision moment after the initial decision, which allows the user to change the initial decision upon feedback. To conceptualize the consequences these two characteristics of real-time feedback have for the decision-making process, we consider a general case where an agent is tasked by a principal with making one (or a series of) decision(s). The agent exerts effort by searching for information and then analyzing and evaluating available decision alternatives. Based on her evaluation of available alternatives, the agent then makes a decision. The main aim of a real-time feedback system in such a setting is to improve the quality of the final (i.e., postfeedback) decision by providing immediate feedback on the agent’s initial (i.e., prefeedback) decision and allowing the agent to correct a wrong initial decision upon feedback. In this second decision moment, real-time feedback serves as a decision aid by providing specific, decision-facilitating information (Casas-Arce et al. 2017b,Costello et al. 2020,Manthei et al. 2023) in the form of corrective feedback on the initial decision. Consequently, real-time feedback can be expected to result in an improvement in the quality of the agent’s final decision relative to their initial decision. However, real-time feedback could also affect the initial (i.e., prefeedback) decision because it changes the context of the initial decision in at least two meaningful ways. First, because feedback is provided immediately after every decision of the agent, a real-time feedback system can be seen as aspotlighton the agent’s decision making in general and wrong initial decisions in particular. The continuous cycle of decisions and immediate feedback can be perceived by the agent as intense scrutiny of their decision making and lead to feelings of anxiety and worry commonly associated with situations where one is being evaluated (Leitenberg 1990,Zeidner and Matthews 2005) and/or generally under increased performance pressure (Apesteguia and Palacios-Huerta 2010). Furthermore, real-time feedback will specifically increase the costs associated with making a wrong initial decision. Individuals generally have the desire to perceive themselves positively and receive positive evaluations, which results in concerns regarding self-presentation and self-enhancement (Jones 1964,1973;Kowalski and Leary 1990). Wrong initial decisions will trigger negative feedback, which is associated with self-confidence/ego (Northcraft and Ashford 1990,VandeWalle et al. 2000,Bénabou and Tirole 2003) and, depending on the publicness of the feedback, potentially additional reputational costs for the agent (Ashford and Cummings 1983,Northcraft and Ashford 1990). In addition, if the agent makes a wrong initial decision, under real-time feedback this will lead to corrective feedback, which in turn creates the additional costs of having to reconsider and revise the initial decision. Second, and in contrast to the perspective of aspotlight, a real-time feedback system can be seen as asafety netagainst wrong initial decisions because it creates the opportunity to correct a wrong initial decision upon feedback. Because real-time feedback offers the possibility to promptly adjust an initial decision before its consequences (fully) manifest, it allows the agent to mitigate (at least some of) the costs associated with making a wrong initial decision. From the perspective of asafety net, real-time feedback would thereby not only reduce the costs of a wrong initial decision but, as a result, also alleviate the agent’s anxiety and worry caused by the pressure to make a correct initial decision. In sum, real-time feedback could, on the one hand, cause negative emotions and increase the costs of wrong initial decisions by introducing aspotlighton the agent’s decision making, but on the other hand, it could also decrease negative emotions and lower the costs of wrong initial decisions by serving as asafety netfor the agent. In the following, we consider which consequences these two perspectives have for the agent’s level of effort and the quality of their initial decision. 2.1. The Effect of Real-Time Feedback on Effort From the perspective of aspotlight, real-time feedback creates the incentive to avoid wrong decisions and, thus, negative feedback. Whereas in most decision contexts incentives are provided as a bonus for good performance, more recent accounting research has alternatively discussed the incentive effect of penalties (Hannan et al. 2005,Church et al. 2008,Hossain and List 2012). Such negative incentives are used less often but can help motivate agents to exert a higher level of effort and increase their utility and performance (Frederickson and Waller 2005,Van der Stede et al. 2020). Real-time feedback involves the pointing out and, to some extent, scrutiny of mistakes immediately after a decision is made. This imposes penalty incentives onto agents who want to avoid being scrutinized for mistakes by the real-time feedback received. The higher costs of making wrong initial decisions under real-time feedback would increase the pressure on the agent to make a correct initial decision. As a result, the agent will increase their effort under real-time feedback as they strive to avoid negative feedback by avoiding wrong initial decisions. From the perspective of asafety net, we would expect that real-time feedback has the opposite effect on effort. The availability of a second decision moment, where the agent can change her decision upon feedback, reduces the costs the agent faces in case of a wrong initial decision and would, therefore, mute the agent’s existing incentives to exert high effort in her information search and analysis when making the initial decision. Consequently, real-time feedback can result in lower effort exerted by agents who perceive it as asafety net. 2.2. The Effect of Real-Time Feedback on the Quality of Initial Decisions From the perspective of aspotlight, real-time feedback could result in worse initial decisions because the perceived scrutiny causes feelings of anxiety and worry, inhibiting the agent’s information processing. According to attention control theory (Eysenck and Calvo 1992,Eysenck et al. 2007), anxiety and worry affect the cognitive system because they shift the allocation of attention away from on-task activities to threat-related stimuli (e.g., worrisome thoughts or other threatening task-irrelevant distractors). This shift of attention will interfere with the cognitive processing of the available information, weighing the different decision alternatives, and deciding the specific course of action. In line with this theory, prior research has provided empirical evidence that performance pressure in general and an individual’s perception of being scrutinized and evaluated in particular can be detrimental to decision making and performance (Baumeister 1984;Lord et al. 1987;Ashton 1990;Beilock et al. 2004;Wilson 2008,2012;Apesteguia and Palacios-Huerta 2010;Lee and Grafton 2015). Consequently, if real-time feedback is perceived as aspotlightby the agent, it could result in worse initial (prefeedback) decisions. From the perspective of asafety net, the opposite argument could be made. Specifically, real-time feedback could result in better initial decisions because it improves cognitive processing. Perceiving real-time feedback as asafety netwill have the opposite effect from viewing it as aspotlightin that it will decrease the perceived stress and pressure associated with making a correct decision initially. Consequently, if agents perceive real-time feedback as asafety net,this could free up cognitive resources and, in turn, result in better initial (prefeedback) decision making. 2.3. The Moderating Effect of Task Experience Based on the discussion above, the effect of real-time feedback on effort and the quality of initial decisions can be positive or negative depending on how individuals perceive it. This notion is consistent with prior research, which suggests that feedback can have different effects on individuals’ motivation and performance depending on contextual factors that affect feedback perception and use (Ilgen et al. 1979,Kluger and DeNisi 1996). Based on the large stream of feedback research that highlights task experience, that is, an agent’s experience with the decision-making task at hand, as a central contextual factor (Andiola 2014,Anseel et al. 2015,Ashford et al. 2016), we posit that an agent’s task experience could drive their view of real-time feedback as aspotlightorsafety net. Agents with little task experience are comparably more likely to suffer from anxiety and pressure when being evaluated or expecting negative feedback (Canal-Bruland et al. 2010,Andiola et al. 2019). Because of their low experience, they typically lack an established reputation of competence and face greater concerns about others’ impressions of their expertise (Leary and Kowalski 1990,Levy et al. 1995,Anseel et al. 2015). Because there is little information available on their past performance, every new performance signal will be weighted more strongly by others when forming an opinion about the agent’s ability compared with experienced agents with an established reputation for whom new signals will be less incrementally informative. As a result, inexperienced agents will perceive a stronger pressure to avoid negative performance signals (negative feedback) than experienced agents. This is further in line with the literature on self-esteem, which posits that individuals who hold comparably less favorable views of themselves (which can be expected of agents with little task experience) have a greater need for esteem enhancement and will, therefore, react more strongly to negative feedback (Dittes 1959,Jones 1973). Because of the differences between experienced and inexperienced agents discussed above, we posit that different aspects of real-time feedback will be differently salient for experienced compared with inexperienced agents. The perceived scrutiny produced by a continuous cycle of decisions and feedback, as well as the self-confidence and possible reputational costs of wrong initial decisions inherent to thespotlightperspective, will be more salient to inexperienced compared with experienced agents. In contrast, the lower costs of wrong initial decisions due to the possibility of adjusting an initial decision upon feedback and the resulting reduction in pressure will be more salient to experienced compared with inexperienced agents. Inexperienced agents are, therefore, more likely to adopt a view of real-time feedback as aspotlight, whereas experienced agents are more likely to adopt the view of real-time feedback as asafety net. 2.4. Hypotheses Based on the above discussion, we expect that the effect of real-time feedback on an agent’s effort will depend on the agent’s level of task experience. An inexperienced agent will perceive the real-time feedback more dominantly as aspotlighton their decision making and respond by increasing their effort in an attempt to avoid negative feedback. In contrast, an experienced agent will perceive real-time feedback more dominantly as asafety netand reduce their effort in response to the lower costs of making wrong initial decisions. Specifically, we hypothesize the following: Real-time feedback has a positive effect on effort for inexperienced agents. Real-time feedback has a negative effect on effort for experienced agents. We also expect differential effects of real-time feedback on the quality of agents’ initial decisions depending on the agent’s level of task experience. An inexperienced agent’s decision making will suffer from anxiety-induced cognitive limitations. Experienced agents, in contrast, will benefit from reduced pressure, which will improve their cognitive processing and decision quality. Specifically, we hypothesize: Real-time feedback has a negative effect on the quality of initial decisions before feedback for inexperienced agents. Real-time feedback has a positive effect on the quality of initial decisions before feedback for experienced agents. These hypotheses are not without tension, considering that in line withHypotheses 1aand1b, we expect that real-time feedback will lead to increased effort for inexperienced individuals and decreased effort for experienced individuals. Effort can generally be expected to be positively related to performance. As a result, inexperienced individuals’ increased effort in information search and analysis might compensate for the decrease in cognitive processing due to perceived pressure. Conversely, experienced individuals’ decreased effort in information search and analysis, which will result in less deliberate decision making, might compensate for the improved cognitive processing due to reduced pressure. Lastly, we expect real-time feedback to have a generally positive effect on the quality of individuals’ final decisions. Specifically, we hypothesize the following: Real-time feedback has a positive effect on the quality of final decisions after feedback for inexperienced and experienced agents. Under the assumption that the real-time feedback system is flawless in that it perfectly points out and allows for correcting every initially wrong decision, this hypothesis is trivial. More likely, however, real-time feedback systems will be imperfect. They will vary in their ability to identify initially wrong decisions, to unambiguously inform individuals, and to allow for a full correction of the initial decision. In such a case,Hypothesis 3is not without tension because the quality of final decisions is a result of the quality of the initial decision, which, in line withHypotheses 2aand2b, can be differential. If, for example, the quality of individuals’ initial decisions suffers because of either decreased effort or reduced cognitive processing to a larger degree than can be corrected by an imperfect real-time feedback mechanism, final decision quality could be equal to, or worse than, that undertraditionalfeedback.
article.3. Institutional Background: 3.1. The German Professional Soccer Championship Our research setting is the German professional soccer championshipBundesliga, organized by the German soccer association (Deutscher Fussball-Bund (DFB)). The championship is organized in a league format consisting of hierarchical divisions. The two highest divisions, which are the focus of our study, consist of 18 teams each, and every season consists of 34 game days. Every game is officiated by the main referee, assisted by two assistant referees. Although the main referee makes all game calls, the assistant referees are positioned at the two long sides of the pitch and can assist with call making while communicating with the main referee via radio earpieces.3Each season, around 40 referees officiate games of the two highest professional divisions. Referees qualify through persistent high performance in lower divisions, participate in extensive training, and regularly have to meet strict physical performance norms. Although the game calendar is usually fixed far in advance, referees are assigned to games on relatively short notice (approximately two days) to minimize the potential for game manipulation because of referee bribing or blackmailing. 3.2. Referees’ Incentives and Performance Evaluation In our setting, referees face financial and nonfinancial (i.e., reputation) incentives. For every season during which a referee officiates games, they receive a fixed salary, which depends on the highest division the referee officiates games in, their tenure, and their status as a FIFA referee. In addition, referees receive an additional payment for every game they officiate, which depends on the division of the respective game. Referees are assigned to games based on previous performance and are regularly promoted or demoted among divisions. Referees who show better performance are typically assigned (i) more games in absolute terms and (ii) relatively more games in the 1stDivision. As a result, referees face substantial implicit incentives to perform well. In addition to financial incentives, referees are rewarded for good performance by being assigned to particularly important and prestigious games outside of the mainBundesligacompetition. For example, it is considered a special honor for a referee to be assigned to the final game of the German Cup or prestigious international competitions such as the FIFA World Cup. Every game is followed by a referee observer assigned by the DFB, who observes the referee’s performance on the pitch and submits a performance evaluation report after the end of a game. This report contains a performance rating as well as an evaluation of the game’s difficulty.4The performance evaluation is discussed between the observer and referee after the end of a game. Thereby, referees receive feedback on their performance after the end of every game they officiate, including comments on decisions made in individual critical situations. 3.3. The VAR Technology Starting with the 2017–2018 season, the DFB has introduced a real-time feedback technology to enable referees to correct wrong game calls. Under the new system, referees continue to make all game calls according to their judgment. However, important calls involving potentially game-changing situations are instantly reviewed by an additional referee who is designated as the VAR. The VAR is positioned in a facility with multiple video screens and has access to video footage from all different angles covered by TV cameras. Additionally, the VAR has access to computer-aided analysis of footage for situations that require assessing the exact positioning of players relative to each other. After reviewing every important call, the VAR provides feedback to the referee via a radio earpiece. If the VAR determines that a call is wrong, she notifies the referee and recommends changing the call. The main referee can either directly make a decision based on the recommendation of the VAR or review the video footage using a video screen in a dedicated review area before deciding whether to change the call or reject the assessment of the VAR. An overview of the process of referee decision making under the VAR system compared with thetraditionalsystem is presented in the Online Appendix in Figure OA1. According to the DFB guidelines, four categories of calls are considered potentially game changing and, therefore, VAR relevant. Firstly, the VAR reviews all goals scored. After a goal, the VAR reviews whether the ball has correctly passed the goal line and whether there was no foul, handball, or offside situation before the goal. Secondly, the VAR reviews penalty kick decisions to determine whether a penalty kick was awarded correctly or whether a penalty kick should have been awarded to a team because of a situation not spotted by the main referee. Thirdly, the VAR reviews the decisions of the referee to send a player off the pitch (red card) and ensures that no unsportsmanlike conduct, which should lead to sending off a player, goes unnoticed. Lastly, the VAR reviews whether, in any decision involving personal penalties (yellow cardandred card), the referee punishes the correct player, and there is no mixing up of players. For the first two seasons, the new system was only used during games of the 1stDivision. On March 21, 2018, the DFB announced that the technology would also be introduced in the 2ndDivision, starting with the 2019–2020 season (Deutsche Fussball Liga 2019).
article.4. Research Design: Our main analysis focuses on the effect of the VAR on three key variables of interest, that is, effort, quality of initial decisions, and quality of final decisions. In our main analysis, we estimate the effects of the VAR on referees’ effort and decision quality for referees with different levels of experience. In additional analyses, we exploit cross-sectional variation in referees’ decisions to gain a better understanding of the mechanisms behind our main results. 4.1. Data and Variables To investigate the effects of the VAR on referee effort and decision quality, we obtain data on referee- and game-specific variables for all games in the 1stand 2ndGerman Soccer Divisions during the two seasons 2016–2017 (pre-VAR introduction) and 2017–2018 (post-VAR introduction). We limit our analysis to one season before and one season after the introduction of the VAR because of the availability of data for our main measure of decision quality (see below). With 306 games per division and season, we collect data on a total of 1,224 referee-game observations. To capture referees’ effort, we rely on their physical effort, which manifests as their movements on the pitch. Although such data are not publicly available, we obtain information on referees’ physical effort through cooperation with the DFB. Specifically, we use three proxies that we discuss and validate with the DFB:DISTANCEcaptures the distance covered by the referee during a game, scaled by the average distance covered by all players.SPEEDis a referee’s average running speed throughout a game in kilometers/hour. Lastly,RUNSis the number of times a referee made a high-intensity run divided by the number of the referee’s accelerations to scale for the ball’s movements that could warrant a referee to run. Although observability of (physical) effort is a key advantage of our setting, it is important to note that the relation between referees’ physical effort and the quality of their decisions is complex. Exerting more physical effort by running more and faster helps referees keep up with the gameplay, thereby allowing them to make calls with a clear view and from a beneficial visual angle for the situation in question (Oudejans et al. 2005,Mallo et al. 2012,Gomez-Carmona and Pino-Ortega 2016). However, exerting physical effort, particularly by sprinting, will also exhaust referees, which could impair their cognitive processing and thereby hamper decision quality. This means that for soccer referees, an increase in physical effort will not always lead to better decisions, which might raise the question of whether physical effort should be an outcome of interest in this specific setting.5However, even if in our setting increasing physical effort will not necessarily help a referee improve the quality of their decisions (and, thus, avoid negative feedback due to a wrong call), exploring how the VAR affects referees’ physical effort is important to empirically test our theory. We theorize that real-time feedback has differential effects on agents depending on their perception of the VAR as asafety netor aspotlight, which will, at least in part, stem from differently strong concerns about others’ impressions of their expertise. Because referees’ physical effort is publicly observable, visibly exerting more effort might be a powerful way to manage the impressions of fans and the referee observer who evaluates the referee’s performance. Testing whether the VAR has differential effects on effort for referees with different levels of experience, therefore, allows us to evaluate support for our theory that inexperienced agents adopt aspotlightview of real-time feedback (which leads them to increase effort to manage others’ impressions of their competence) whereas experienced agents tend to adopt asafety netview (leading them to reduce effort for the reasons described in our hypothesis building, as these referees face no pressure to engage in impression management). To capture referees’ decision quality, we count how many of the calls the referee made in VAR-relevant situations (i.e., goal, penalty, red card, mix-up of players) were wrong, relying on analyses from the sports forumWahretabelle(www.wahretabelle.de).Wahretabelleis a large online forum where users can put important calls by referees in the 1stand 2ndGerman Soccer Divisions up for public discussion if they feel the call is debatable. After a discussion in the forum, members of an expert panel of selected forum moderators with demonstrated experience and impartiality cast a vote on whether the referee’s decision in the respective situation was correct or not.6We code game calls as wrong if they have been put up for discussion and classified as wrong following the majority vote of the expert panel. Additionally, we record initial calls in VAR games as wrong if a call was adjusted by the referee after VAR feedback and the final decision was not determined to be wrong by theWahretabelleforum. For this, several research assistants who are not part of the author team collect data on VAR interventions using match reports from the archive of the sports magazineKicker(www.kicker.de) and game footage. The research assistants then record for each relevant situation the referee’s initial call, whether the VAR intervened, and the final call after potential VAR intervention. We construct three measures of referees’ decision quality:WRONGis the number of wrong calls made by the referee in a game in situations that concern a goal, penalty, red card, or mix-up of players.WRONG_RELis a relative measure of wrong calls calculated as the number of wrong calls scaled by the number of situations where a referee potentially had to make a decision, calculated as the sum of fouls, shots on target, and offside situations.7Finally,WRONG_GCcaptures the number of wrong game-changing calls made, that is, wrong calls that changed the score of a game (home team in the lead, away team in the lead, or draw) at the moment of the decision.8For each of these three decision quality measures, we differentiate between the initial decision before a potential VAR intervention and the final call after a potential VAR intervention and a call reversal. Consequently,WRONG_INIT,WRONG_REL_INIT, andWRONG_GC_INITcapture the quality of initial calls made by referees before a potential VAR intervention, andWRONG_FIN,WRONG_REL_FIN, andWRONG_GC_FINcapture the quality of referees’ final calls after a potential VAR intervention.9 To account for differences in games that might make them more or less difficult to officiate, we include two control variables. First, we control for the difference in strengths between the competing teams based on the idea that games between opponents of relatively equal strength will be closer and, therefore, more challenging to officiate, as any mistake by the referee could potentially affect the outcome of the game. We calculateGAME_CLOSEby dividing the pregame betting odds of the bookmakerBet365for the stronger team by the odds for the weaker team.GAME_CLOSEthen takes values between zero and one, where values close to zero mean that one team is substantially stronger (i.e., the game is not expected to be close) and values closer to one mean that the teams are similarly strong (i.e., the game is expected to be close). Second, we include a control that picks up high-stakes games as such games tend to be more heated and competitive. Specifically, we use the indicatorGAME_IMPORTANT, which takes a value of one for (i) games between top teams; (ii) placement games during the last quarter of the season, that is, games between teams directly competing with each other for the championship, relegation from the 1stDivision (2ndDivision) to the 2ndDivision (3rdDivision), or promotion from the 2ndDivision to the 1stDivision; and (iii) derbies between local rivals, that is, games between teams from cities in close proximity to each other.10 To test whether the effects of the VAR differ between referees with different levels of task experience, we use the indicatorEXPERIENCED, which takes a value of one for referees with above-median experience calculated as the number of years since their career started as a referee in professional soccer at the beginning of our sample period, and zero otherwise. A complete list of all variables with definitions and data sources, including referee- and match-specific variables obtained from public sports databases, is provided in the Online Appendix. 4.2. Method For our analysis, we exploit the fact that referees from the same pool are assigned to officiate games in the 1stand 2ndDivisions, but the VAR system was introduced only for games in the 1stDivision. Consequently, we treat games in the 1stDivision as a quasi-experimental treatment group while using games in the 2ndDivision as a control group. We then use a difference-in-difference approach to identify the effects of the VAR. Because referees officiate a mix of games across divisions, a referee’s assignment to treatment or control group is not fixed over time but changes every game day. To estimate the effect of the VAR on referee effort and decision quality, we use the following specification: DVitis the effort or decision quality, respectively, of refereeiin gamet. VAR_GROUPiis an indicator taking the value of one for games in the 1stDivision. VAR_PERIODtis an indicator taking the value of one for games in the 2017–2018 season. CONTROLSitis a vector of control variables. δiis a vector of indicators to capture individual referee-fixed effects. The rationale for controlling for referee-fixed effects is that general ability, experience, and level of physical fitness might allow referees to perform better.11Furthermore, to account for the possibility that referees’ performance affects performance in the future and, therefore, observations within referees are not independently and identically distributed, we allow for standard errors clustered by referee. To investigate whether the VAR has a different effect depending on a referee’s level of experience, we estimate our main model (Equation (1)) separately for subsamples of referees with above-median experience (EXPERIENCED= 1) and below-median experience (EXPERIENCED= 0).
article.5. Results: 5.1. Descriptive Statistics Table 1reports descriptive statistics for the total sample. On average, referees cover a distance that is very close to that of an average player (mean of 1.003, representing 10.461 km (6.500 miles)) at an average speed of 6.658 km/h (4.137 mph). The sample referees vary in their experience (mean of 9.183 years and SD of 4.379 years), whereas their physique (height, weight, body mass index (BMI)) is relatively similar. The game-specific statistics show that referees make a large number of decisions, with on average 2.725 goals scored in a game out of 8.616 shots on target, 29.313 fouls committed, and 3.444 offside situations per game. Referees make on average 0.448 wrong initial decisions, approximately half of which are potentially game changing. Validation of the Identification Strategy A critical identifying assumption of our difference-in-difference design is that referee effort and decision quality would have moved in parallel in the treatment and control group absent the VAR technology treatment. Because counterfactuals are unobservable, it is not possible to formally test whether this assumption holds. To alleviate some of the concerns that the treatment and control groups follow different trends, we follow prior research (e.g.,Cascino et al. 2020) and test for differences in trends before the treatment. Specifically, we estimate a specification where instead of a VAR treatment indicator, we include separate event-time dummies, each marking a quarter of a season relative to the VAR introduction (t= 0), and plot the estimated treatment effects. We omit the indicator fort− 1, which serves as the benchmark period. Results presented inFigure 1((a) to (c), referee effort; (d) to (f), decision quality) suggest that firstly, treatment effects before the adoption of the VAR are generally not statistically significantly different from zero, and secondly, we observe no evidence of differential trends in any of our dependent variables across 1st(treated) and 2nd(control) Divisions over time. Collectively, these results provide no indication that the parallel-trend assumption is violated in our setting. Another assumption of our identification strategy is that any differences observed in referee performance between VAR and no-VAR games result from a change in referee judgment as opposed to a change in game characteristics. In other words, a critical assumption is that the presence of the VAR system during games does not affect players’ behavior in a way that would make game calling more or less difficult for referees. This could be the case if players commit fewer punishable offenses because the VAR increases the likelihood that a punishable offense will be discovered and called by the referee or the VAR. To evaluate the possibility that our results are confounded by a contemporaneous change in player behavior, we consider the plausibility of such a change in situations that are reviewed by the VAR, that is, offside and foul situations preceding a goal, fouls punishable by a penalty kick, and fouls punishable by a red card. To correctly judge whether a player is in an offside position, the pass-giving, pass-receiving, and last-positioned defending player all need to be observed at a sufficiently small visual angle, implying that larger distances to the gameplay are more beneficial in judging offside situations (Huettermann et al. 2017). Therefore, it is implausible that an offside situation could be judged (and actively influenced) by a passing or pass-receiving player more reliably than by a professional referee with the advantage of a more beneficial visual angle. To consider the possibility that players commit fewer fouls in response to the VAR, we estimate difference-in-difference models as described inEquation (1)using players’ number of tackles and number of fouls, as well as the numbers of yellow and red cards for punished offenses, as dependent variables. Untabulated results show no effect of the VAR on tackles or yellow or red cards that is statistically significant at any conventional confidence level. For fouls, we find a positive effect of the VAR, suggesting that, if anything, players commit more instead of fewer fouls in VAR games, which makes it implausible that such a development is caused by the VAR.12Therefore, collectively, we find no support for the possible concern that changes in referee performance observed during VAR games are driven by contemporaneous changes in player behavior rather than the effect of the VAR on referee performance itself. In addition to validating the assumptions of our identification strategy, we conduct robustness checks to ensure that our results are not confounded by heterogeneity in the assignment of referees to games. It is unlikely that referees are assigned to 1stand 2ndDivision games entirely randomly. We, therefore, test the robustness of our results using coarsened exact matching (CEM) (Iacus et al. 2012), where we limit our analyses to a group of referees with equal pretreatment characteristics. We match referees by experience, FIFA referee status, weight, and height and perform our main analyses using the matched sample. Although this reduces our sample size by about one-third, untabulated analyses show results that are similar to our main specification. 5.3. The Effect of the VAR on Referee Effort and Decision Quality Table 4presents results of the analysis of the effect of the VAR on referee effort. In line withHypotheses 1aand1b, we expect to find a positive effect of the VAR on effort for less experienced referees and a negative effect of the VAR on effort for more experienced referees. To evaluate support for our hypotheses, we interpret the coefficientβ3inEquation (1), that is, the difference-in-difference estimator captured in the interactionVAR_GROUP × VAR_PERIOD. In line with our expectations, we find that the effect of the VAR on referee effort depends on the referee’s level of experience. Results presented in columns 4–6 suggest that in VAR games, less experienced referees increase their effort by increasing the number of high-intensity runs (RUNS[β= 0.007,p< 0.05]) with a second measure (average speed) being close to marginal significance (SPEED[β= 0.093,p= 0.138]). In contrast, results presented in columns 7–9 show that more experienced referees reduce their effort under the VAR, with the coefficient on the difference-in-difference estimator being negative and significant at least at the 10% level for all three measures of effort. Chi-square tests further suggest that the coefficients on the difference-in-difference estimator are different between the two subsamples for all three proxies of effort at least at the 5% level. In sum, these results provide some support forHypothesis 1aand strong support forHypothesis 1b. In sum, these results show partial support forHypothesis 3, suggesting that under the VAR, there is an improvement in the quality of final decisions only for more experienced but not for less experienced referees.13Importantly, the statistically insignificant effect of the VAR on the quality of final decisions for less experienced referees does not mean that the VAR does not help correct the wrong calls of these referees. article.6. Additional Analyses: After analyzing the effects of the VAR on referees’ effort and decision quality, we conduct additional analyses to test some of the assumptions of our theoretical arguments and to provide more context to the effects we document. 6.1. Insights from Interviews with Referees To complement our analysis of archival data with qualitative evidence from the field (Soltes 2014,Bloomfield et al. 2016), we have conducted semistructured interviews with five DFB referees who officiate games in the two highest divisions.15In particular, we were interested in their perception of the VAR as a real-time feedback system. Most referees stated that they perceive the VAR as a helpful way to avoid wrong final game calls. One example that sums up statements in this vein quite well is “For me, the VAR is and was a safeguard. This means that I can no longer decide the game by making a catastrophically wrong decision. For me, the VAR is a safety net that provides an additional level of security.” However, the interviewees also admitted that they knew of colleagues who felt pressured by the potentially corrective feedback, making statements such as “It all depends on how self-confident you are. Some referees tend to justify their decision [to the VAR] in order not to be corrected”; “I noticed that some referees are saying: please don’t send me out [to the video review area].16My credo, on the other hand, is: please send me out. I want to do it right. In the end, the right decision counts”; and “The outside view is of course that, at the end of the day, it’s about the right decision—be it with or without VAR assistance. But internally, I can tell you, if I’m being called out to the video review area, I know I have made a big mistake. And if I’m standing out there 20 times in 10 games, then I really have to ask myself what’s going wrong there.” In sum, our interviews provide two important insights. First, the interviews provide anecdotal evidence for the theoretical arguments on which we base our hypotheses. Whereas some referees perceive the VAR as added pressure, others see it as a safety net that reduces pressure. This insight is important because it raises our confidence that our theory-driven expectations concerning possible mechanisms of real-time feedback are appropriate and that the VAR setting is suitable for obtaining theoretically generalizable results. Second, the interviews provide anecdotal evidence for the notion that the effects of the VAR depend on referees’ specific attitudes toward the VAR. Although neither of the interviewees directly pointed to referee experience as the main driver behind referees’ specific attitudes toward the VAR, this insight is important because it supports our expectation of differential effects of real-time feedback and, therefore, the importance of considering contextual factors. 6.2. Analysis of the Type of Wrong Initial Decisions Referees Make Referees’ decisions can be generally categorized as (i) decisions to interrupt the gameplay (e.g., decisions to call a situation in question afoul) and (ii) decisions to continue the gameplay (e.g., decisions to call the situation in questionno foul). Consequently, referees’ wrong calls can be subdivided into two types: errors by omission, that is, wrong decisions to not interrupt the game (e.g., wrongly not to award a penalty because a foul was overlooked), and errors by commission, that is, wrong decisions to interrupt the game (e.g., to wrongly award a penalty after a situation was incorrectly assessed as a foul). For the interpretation of the results of our main analysis, it is important to differentiate between these two types of errors because under the VAR, they are differently easy to correct and, therefore, differently problematic. Without the VAR, both types of errors are more or less equally problematic as neither can be corrected in the absence of real-time feedback. In VAR games, however, errors by omission become preferential relative to errors by commission because their impact on the game can be corrected more easily. In the case of an omission, the counterfactual (i.e., how a situation would have unfolded without the referee’s wrong decision) is known, whereas in the case of a commission, the counterfactual and, thus, the exact impact of the referee’s wrong call on the game are unknown. If a referee wrongly does not interrupt the gameplay and allows a situation to unfold (e.g., overlooks a foul of the attacking team before the team scores a goal), this error can be corrected after the VAR feedback by disallowing the goal. If, however, the referee wrongly interrupts the gameplay (e.g., because of an apparent foul of the attacking team) and is then notified by the VAR of the error, it is unclear how the original situation would have unfolded (e.g., whether the attacking team would have scored a goal). As a result, the VAR changes the optimal (i.e., desirable from the perspective of the principal) strategy of referees for close calls in difficult-to-judge situations and makes it preferential to not interrupt the gameplay, risking an error of omission rather than interrupting the gameplay and risking an error of commission. With respect to the results of our main analysis, this means that if in VAR games, referees generally let more situations play out and specifically, the increase in the number of wrong initial decisions we observe for less experienced referees is driven by errors by omission, this might be relatively unproblematic and perhaps even economically preferential. To explore this possibility, we code for all situations put up for discussion in theWahretabelleforum as “debatable” whether the initial decision made by the referee was to continue or interrupt the gameplay. We further code for all wrong calls made by referees whether the error was one of omission or commission. In a first set of analyses, we test whether referees are generally more hesitant to interrupt the gameplay in VAR games. For this, we create the variableINTERRUPT, which takes a value of one when the referee interrupted the gameplay in a debatable situation and zero if the referee let the situation play out. Analogously to our measures for wrong decisions, we then createINTERRUPT_RELandINTERRUPT_GCto capture interruptions relative to the number of decisions to make and the number of interruptions in potentially game-changing situations. We then estimateEquation (1)using these three measures as alternative dependent variables. Subsequently, we test in a second set of analyses whether the VAR specifically affects the type of wrong calls referees make. For this, we repeat our main analysis but instead of using measures of decision quality that are based on all errors, we use separate measures that pick up only errors by omission and errors by commission, respectively. We present the results of both sets of analyses inTable 7. Results for interruptions, which are presented in panel A, suggest that contrary to the optimal behavior of letting situations play out, referees interrupt the gameplay more often in VAR games. Specifically, we find positive coefficients on the interactionVAR_GROUP×VAR_PERIODin almost all specifications across the full sample and both subsamples of more and less experienced referees. When focusing on errors by omission, untabulated results show no statistically significant evidence for an effect of the VAR on either referees’ initial or final decision quality for either more experienced or less experienced referees. However, results presented in panel B ofTable 7suggest that the effect of the VAR on the quality of less experienced referees’ initial decisions documented in our main analysis is driven by errors by commission. Specifically, Models 4–6 show a positive effect of the VAR on the number of wrong decisions made by less experienced referees for two of our three proxies of decision quality. At the same time, Models 7–9 show no statistically significant support for an effect of the VAR on errors by commission made by experienced referees. These results are important for at least two reasons. First, they suggest that the higher number of wrong decisions that less experienced referees make under the VAR is indeed problematic because it is driven by errors by commission which are not easily corrected with the help of the VAR. Second, these results suggest that in VAR games, referees in general take higher risks when making their initial decisions. Although, from an economic perspective, we would expect that all referees will use the VAR technology optimally by adjusting their decisions to shift errors from commission to omission, referees instead interrupt the gameplay more often, which, for less experienced referees, results in more errors by commission. One possible explanation for this is that referees in general aim to improve their reputation (Morrison and Bies 1991;Anseel et al. 2007,2015;Ashford et al. 2016;Bolino et al. 2016), whereas inexperienced referees in particular will face greater concerns about others’ impressions and will therefore perceive a stronger pressure to manage their reputation (Leary and Kowalski 1990,Levy et al. 1995,Anseel et al. 2015). Therefore, referees in general, and inexperienced referees in particular, might engage in assertive impression management tactics (Morrison and Bies 1991,Bolino et al. 2008) that involve taking assertive actions that can showcase their competence (Jones and Pittman 1983,Zeelenberg et al. 2002,Moss et al. 2003,Bolino et al. 2008,Anseel et al. 2015). That is, inexperienced referees could shift their focus away from primarily choosing the correct alternative to primarily choosing the alternative that makes their decision making stand out and look more assertive. This finding is also in line with our result on referee effort, which indicates that inexperienced referees, who are more likely to perceive the VAR as aspotlight, might increase their impression management effort by running harder and faster so as to appear to be a conscientious referee. In other words, inexperienced referees appear to choose action rather than inaction to manage impressions both in their effort choice and in their initial decision making. These insights are consistent with evidence from the economics literature on judicial decision making, which highlights that although judges face reputational concerns if their decisions are overturned in appeals, their irrational decision making can often be explained by self-advertisement or self-aggrandizement (Posner 1990,Miceli and Coşgel 1994). To further examine the possibility that under the VAR, referees try to make primarilyassertiverather thancorrectdecisions, we conduct a set of analyses where we explore the effect of the VAR on wrong calls that favor the away as opposed to the home team. In our analyses, which we describe in detail and tabulate in the Online Appendix, we find further evidence consistent with the notion ofassertiverather thancorrectdecision making for less experienced referees. Before the VAR introduction, we find that, in line with conventional wisdom, referees’ decision making is on average biased toward making wrong calls that favor the home rather than the away team. In VAR games, we find no evidence that less experienced referees reduce the number of wrong calls favoring the home team but find strong evidence that they increase the number of wrong calls in favor of the away team. This is in line with the aim to appear assertive because by deciding close situations in favor of the away team, the referee goes seemingly against the pressure of the crowd and the stereotype of a home bias in referee decision making. For more experienced referees, in contrast, we find a reduction of wrong calls in favor of the home team with some evidence for a simultaneous increase in wrong calls favoring the away team. Thus, the distribution of errors made by experienced referees becomes more balanced, consistent with more experienced referees viewing the VAR as asafety net, which alleviates pressure from the crowd to decide in favor of the home team. 6.3. Analysis of Referee Learning To provide additional insight into the effects of the VAR, we exploit longitudinal variation to explore whether referees show learning with respect to their behavior in VAR games. In particular, we test whether the effect of the VAR on referee effort and decision-making quality changes over time by repeating our main analysis separately for the first compared with the second half of the season. Results, which we tabulate and discuss in more detail in the Online Appendix, suggest that the increase in effort and decrease in the quality of initial decisions that we document for less experienced referees in our main analysis are concentrated in early games after the introduction of the VAR. Specifically, we find statistically significant evidence for higher effort and more wrong initial calls for less experienced referees only in the first half of the VAR season. During the second half of the VAR season, as referees gain experience working with the VAR, we no longer find statistically significant evidence for any of these effects. These results are consistent with the notion that less experienced referees view the VAR initially as aspotlightbut learn to handle the pressure and perceived scrutiny as they gain experience with the system. For more experienced referees, our results suggest that the reduction of effort and improvement of the quality of final calls are concentrated in later games. Specifically, we find some evidence of lower effort in VAR games during both halves of the VAR season but a stronger effect of the VAR in later games. With respect to the quality of final calls, we find no statistically significant evidence for an effect of the VAR in the first half of the VAR season and a reduction in the number of wrong calls in later games. 6.4. Other Boundary Conditions Finally, we make use of the data richness of our setting and explore other possible contextual factors that could moderate the effects of real-time feedback on referees beyond referee experience. In additional analyses, which we tabulate in the Online Appendix, we find no support for a moderating effect of crowd size, stadium capacity, referee FIFA status, referees’ cognitive ability measured as a referee having (or not having) obtained at least a college-level academic degree, or referees’ pre-VAR performance.
article.7. Conclusion: We exploit a natural quasi-experiment in German professional soccer to study the causal effects of real-time feedback on individuals’ effort and performance. In particular, we hypothesize and empirically document that real-time feedback has differential effects on individuals’ effort, quality of initial decisions before feedback, and quality of final decisions after feedback, depending on individuals’ level of experience. Our results allow three important conclusions. First, our results suggest that the prospect of receiving potentially corrective feedback immediately after an initial decision affects the level of effort. On the one hand, for individuals who perceive real-time feedback as aspotlight, it can create penalty incentives that elicit higher effort. On the other hand, for individuals who perceive real-time feedback as asafety net, it can mute existing incentives to exert high effort. Which of the two possible perspectives prevails depends on the individual’s task experience. Second, our results suggest that real-time feedback can have detrimental effects on the quality of decision making with respect to initial decisions before feedback. Although this finding is in line with prior research on the performance effect of highly frequent and timely feedback (Thornock 2016,Casas-Arce et al. 2017a), it stands in stark contrast to practitioner claims (e.g.,Sikder 2017,Fankhauser 2020,Impraise 2020). In addition to finding no support for a positive effect of real-time feedback on initial decision quality, we find that the effect can be negative for some individuals. In this sense, real-time feedback can not only hinder performance improvements, as suggested byCasas-Arce et al. (2017a), but even decrease performance because of feelings of anxiety and worry caused by perceived intense scrutiny and pressure. Third, our results suggest that real-time feedback can be beneficial for decision making through the possibility of adjusting a wrong initial decision upon feedback before the negative consequences of the decision (fully) manifest. By providing feedback on an initial decision immediately after the decision was made, real-time feedback creates a second decision moment. The feedback information on the initial decision thereby serves as a decision aid for the second decision and facilitates a corrected second decision. As long as a prompt reversal can avoid at least some negative consequences of a wrong initial course of action, real-time feedback can, therefore, result in overall improved performance with respect to final decisions after feedback. Our results inform the debate on technology-aided decision making by highlighting both intended and unintended benefits, as well as drawbacks of systems that provide information ex post immediately after an initial decision has been made. To the extent to which such systems allow for a wrong initial course of action to be corrected before negative consequences (fully) manifest, our results provide empirical support for their usefulness. At the same time, our results caution that providing an ex post decision aid can have unintended repercussions on the ex ante effort and quality with which the initial decision is made. When drawing conclusions from our results, two aspects of our setting need to be kept in mind. First, our analyses are based on the entire population of games and referees in the two highest German professional soccer divisions over two seasons rather than a random sample from this population.Abadie et al. (2020)demonstrate that in such cases, conventional analyses might overestimate standard errors, meaning that the levels of statistical significance we report are potentially too conservative. Although this raises our confidence in the results we report as statistically significant, it could mean that in some cases, we find effects to be not statistically significant when in fact they are. Second, our results are obtained in a highly competitive setting where effort and performance are transparent, can substantially affect decision makers’ career outcomes, and can have substantial financial implications for the involved organizations. We, therefore, expect that similar to other recent studies in accounting that utilize a sports context (e.g.,Black and Vance 2021,Eyring et al. 2021), our findings are particularly relevant in professional environments that are characterized by competition and high-performance pressure. Furthermore, several characteristics of our setting should motivate future research to replicate our findings in other contexts. First, our setting is characterized by public observability of decisions and feedback. Although the feedback referees receive from the VAR is private, it can generally be inferred from the referees’ actions (video review and/or change of an initial call), making it indirectly public. It is, therefore, possible that in particular the negative effects of the VAR we document for less experienced referees are strengthened by the public nature of the VAR feedback, and therefore more intense perceived scrutiny. In additional analyses, we do not find that the effects of the VAR on referee effort and the quality of initial decisions vary with public attention as captured by crowd size and stadium capacity for either the overall sample of referees or the subsamples of less or more experienced referees. Therefore, although we observe no evidence that our results are driven by the public nature of the VAR feedback, we cannot rule out that private versus public observability of decisions and feedback is an important contextual factor that affects the effects of real-time feedback on effort and decision quality. Second, even though the feedback referees receive from the VAR can generally be inferred from their actions, our setting does not allow us to observe it directly. Anecdotally (i.e., based on our interviews with the responsible experts at the DFB and the referees), our understanding is that whenever the VAR suggests a change to a call, referees will follow this recommendation, trusting the VAR’s superior insight that stems from access to replays from different camera angles and computer-assisted offside analysis. However, as we cannot directly observe the feedback, our analysis cannot speak to whether VAR feedback is sometimes ignored/overruled by referees and to what extent referees’ propensity to do so might differ for more versus less experienced referees. Third, although in our setting, agents receive both positive and negative feedback, negative feedback signals are more salient and impactful. The VAR reviews all calls referees make in VAR-relevant situations. In the case of a wrong initial decision, referees will receive negative feedback from the VAR. In the case that an initial call is correct, referees either will receive positive feedback from the VAR or at least will be able to infer the positive feedback indirectly from the lack of intervention by the VAR. Because of the nature of the referee’s task, it is the implicit benchmark that all (important) calls will be made correctly. As a result, the negative feedback referees receive after a wrong initial call will be more salient than the positive feedback received after a correct call. By replicating our analysis in a different setting, future research could explore to what extent the differential salience of positive compared with negative feedback signals is a potentially important boundary condition in the relationship between real-time feedback and effort and performance. Fourth, as noted earlier, our setting is characterized by a complex relation between effort and performance. This raises the question of how our results for the effect of the VAR on referees’ effort are linked to our results for the quality of referees’ initial decisions and whether this link is specific to our setting. Importantly, our additional analyses show that less experienced referees do not simply make more wrong calls but change their decision-making strategy, specifically making more errors of commission and errors in favor of away teams. Consequently, our result of worse initial calls made by less experienced referees cannot be simply the result of an increase in effort beyond the optimal point, directly resulting in worse decisions. Nevertheless, this characteristic of our setting offers an opportunity for future research to examine our findings in settings with a more clearly positive link between effort and performance.
